annotations.label: Linear programming (formal definition)

anchor.snippet: linear programming is a technique for the optimization of a linear objective function

annotations.note: Mathlib has no linear-program object; Mathlib/Analysis/Convex/Cone/Basic.lean explicitly lists defining linear programs as future work.

annotations.label: Feasible region as convex polytope

anchor.snippet: Its feasible region is a convex polytope

annotations.note: Mathlib has Convex sets and convexHull but no polytope definition nor an LP feasible-region construction.

annotations.label: Standard form of a linear program

anchor.snippet: Linear programs are problems that can be expressed in standard form as

annotations.note: No standard-form LP structure exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Objective function

anchor.snippet: is called the objective function

annotations.note: Linear functionals exist (LinearMap) but no LP objective-function notion is defined.

annotations.label: Standard form (three parts)

anchor.snippet: Standard form is the usual and most intuitive form of describing a linear programming problem

annotations.note: No LP standard-form definition is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Reduction of other forms to standard form

anchor.snippet: can always be rewritten into an equivalent problem in standard form

annotations.note: No LP forms are defined, so no equivalence/reduction theorem exists.

annotations.label: Farmer wheat/barley LP

anchor.snippet: Suppose that a farmer has a piece of farm land

annotations.note: This concrete LP example is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Augmented (slack) form

anchor.snippet: Linear programming problems can be converted into an augmented form

annotations.note: No slack/augmented-form LP construction exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Farmer LP in augmented form

anchor.snippet: The example above is converted into the following augmented form

annotations.note: This specific augmented-form example is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Primal and dual problem

anchor.snippet: Every linear programming problem, referred to as a primal problem, can be converted into a dual problem

annotations.note: ConvexCone/ProperCone duals exist, but LP primal/dual is listed as future work in Mathlib/Analysis/Convex/Cone/Basic.lean.

annotations.label: Dual of the dual is the primal

anchor.snippet: the dual of a dual linear program is the original primal linear program

annotations.note: Mathlib proves the bipolar (double-dual) theorem for proper cones, the cone analog, but not for linear programs.

annotations.label: Weak duality theorem

anchor.snippet: The weak duality theorem states that the objective function value of the dual at any feasible solution is always greater than or equal to

annotations.note: Mathlib/Analysis/Convex/Cone/Basic.lean lists proving weak duality for cone/linear programs as future work.

annotations.label: Strong duality theorem

anchor.snippet: The strong duality theorem states that if the primal has an optimal solution

annotations.note: Strong duality (and the Farkas lemma it needs) is listed as future work in Mathlib/Analysis/Convex/Cone/Basic.lean.

annotations.label: Unbounded primal implies infeasible dual

anchor.snippet: if the primal is unbounded then the dual is infeasible

annotations.note: No LP duality theory is formalized, so this consequence is absent.

annotations.label: Covering LP

anchor.snippet: A covering LP is a linear program of the form

annotations.note: Covering LPs are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Packing LP (dual of covering LP)

anchor.snippet: The dual of a covering LP is a packing LP

annotations.note: Packing LPs are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Packing and covering LP relaxations

anchor.snippet: the LP relaxations of the set packing problem

annotations.note: LP relaxations of set packing/covering are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Fractional coloring as covering LP

anchor.snippet: Finding a fractional coloring of a graph is another example of a covering LP

annotations.note: Fractional coloring is not formulated as an LP in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Complementary slackness theorem

anchor.snippet: Suppose that x = ( x 1 , x 2 , ... , x n ) is primal feasible

annotations.note: Complementary slackness depends on LP duality, which is not formalized.

annotations.label: Linearity implies local optima are global

anchor.snippet: A linear function is a convex function , which implies that every local minimum is a global minimum

annotations.note: Mathlib proves a local minimum of a convex function is a global minimum, which generalizes the linear case.

annotations.label: Optimal solution need not exist

anchor.snippet: An optimal solution need not exist, for two reasons

annotations.note: This meta-statement about LP infeasibility/unboundedness is not formalized.

annotations.label: Optimum attained on boundary when bounded

anchor.snippet: if a feasible solution exists and if the constraint set is bounded, then the optimum value is always attained on the boundary

annotations.note: Mathlib has a maximum-modulus boundary principle for holomorphic maps but no Bauer-type result that a linear functional attains its optimum on the boundary/extreme points of a compact convex set.

annotations.label: Convex combination of solutions is a solution

anchor.snippet: if there are two distinct solutions, then every convex combination of the solutions is a solution

annotations.note: Mathlib proves sublevel sets of a convex function are convex; the optimal set is such a sublevel set, but this LP-specific corollary is not stated.

annotations.label: Basic feasible solutions (vertices)

anchor.snippet: The vertices of the polytope are also called basic feasible solutions

annotations.note: Mathlib defines extreme points (the geometric vertices) but not basic feasible solutions in the LP sense.

annotations.label: Fundamental theorem of linear inequalities (vertices)

anchor.snippet: the fundamental theorem of linear inequalities implies (for feasible problems) that for every vertex

annotations.note: The fundamental theorem of linear inequalities is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Simplex algorithm

anchor.snippet: The simplex algorithm , developed by George Dantzig in 1947, solves LP problems by constructing a feasible solution at a vertex

annotations.note: A simplex-style routine exists internally in the linarith oracle (Mathlib.Tactic.Linarith.Oracle.SimplexAlgorithm) but the simplex algorithm is not formalized as a verified LP solver.

annotations.label: Simplex efficient on random problems

anchor.snippet: The simplex algorithm has been proved to solve "random" problems efficiently, i.e. in a cubic number of steps

annotations.note: Average-case complexity of the simplex method is not formalized.

annotations.label: Klee–Minty exponential worst case

anchor.snippet: Klee and Minty constructed a family of linear programming problems for which the simplex method takes a number of steps exponential in the problem size

annotations.note: The Klee–Minty worst-case construction is not formalized.

annotations.label: Criss-cross algorithm

anchor.snippet: the criss-cross algorithm is a basis-exchange algorithm that pivots between bases

annotations.note: The criss-cross algorithm is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Worst case on Klee–Minty cube

anchor.snippet: Both algorithms visit all 2 D corners of a (perturbed) cube in dimension D

annotations.note: Worst-case behavior on the Klee–Minty cube is not formalized.

annotations.label: Interior-point methods

anchor.snippet: interior-point methods move through the interior of the feasible region

annotations.note: Interior-point methods are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Ellipsoid method polynomial time

anchor.snippet: This is the first worst-case polynomial-time algorithm ever found for linear programming

annotations.note: The ellipsoid method and its polynomial-time bound are not formalized.

annotations.label: Karmarkar's improved polynomial bound

anchor.snippet: improved on Khachiyan's

annotations.note: Karmarkar's algorithm and complexity bound are not formalized.

annotations.label: Vaidya 1987 running time

anchor.snippet: In 1987, Vaidya proposed an algorithm that runs in

annotations.note: Vaidya's 1987 running-time result is not formalized.

annotations.label: Vaidya 1989 running time

anchor.snippet: In 1989, Vaidya developed an algorithm that runs in

annotations.note: Vaidya's 1989 running-time result is not formalized.

annotations.label: Lee–Sidford input sparsity time

anchor.snippet: In 2015, Lee and Sidford showed that linear programming can be solved in

annotations.note: The Lee–Sidford complexity result is not formalized.

annotations.label: Cohen–Lee–Song matrix-multiplication time

anchor.snippet: In 2019, Cohen, Lee and Song improved the running time to

annotations.note: The Cohen–Lee–Song complexity result is not formalized.

annotations.label: Polytopes have subexponential diameter

anchor.snippet: It has been proved that all polytopes have subexponential diameter

annotations.note: Polytope diameter bounds are not formalized (no polytope diameter notion exists).

annotations.label: Arrangement polytope graphs have small diameter

anchor.snippet: graphs of arrangement polytopes are known to have small diameter

annotations.note: Diameter of arrangement-polytope graphs is not formalized.

annotations.label: Integer (linear) programming

anchor.snippet: If all of the unknown variables are required to be integers, then the problem is called an integer programming

annotations.note: Integer programming is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Integer programming is NP-hard

anchor.snippet: integer programming problems are in many practical situations (those with bounded variables) NP-hard

annotations.note: Mathlib has no complexity-class theory (NP-hardness), so this is absent.

annotations.label: 0–1 (binary) integer programming

anchor.snippet: 0–1 integer programming or binary integer programming (BIP) is the special case of integer programming where variables are required to be 0 or 1

annotations.note: 0–1 integer programming is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Mixed integer programming

anchor.snippet: If only some of the unknown variables are required to be integers, then the problem is called a mixed integer (linear) programming

annotations.note: Mixed integer programming is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Totally unimodular subclasses solvable

anchor.snippet: some important subclasses of IP and MIP problems that are efficiently solvable, most notably problems where the constraint matrix is totally unimodular

annotations.note: Mathlib defines totally unimodular matrices but proves nothing about efficient solvability of the associated programs.

annotations.label: Integral linear program

anchor.snippet: A linear program in real variables is said to be integral if it has at least one optimal solution which is integral

annotations.note: Integral linear programs are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Integral polyhedron

anchor.snippet: is said to be integral if for all bounded feasible objective functions c

annotations.note: Integral polyhedra are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Edmonds–Giles equivalent characterization

anchor.snippet: As observed by Edmonds and Giles in 1977, one can equivalently say that the polyhedron

annotations.note: The Edmonds–Giles characterization of integral polyhedra is not formalized.

annotations.label: Convex hull of solutions is integral polyhedron

anchor.snippet: the convex hull of the solutions is an integral polyhedron

annotations.note: convexHull exists in Mathlib but the integral-polyhedron statement about it is not formalized.

annotations.label: Total unimodularity implies integrality

anchor.snippet: One common way of proving that a polyhedron is integral is to show that it is totally unimodular

annotations.note: Total unimodularity is defined, but the Hoffman–Kruskal implication that it yields integral polyhedra is not formalized.
